title: Order Management System (OMS) — System Design
description: Design the OMS that drives an e-commerce order from placement to delivery (and returns), coordinating inventory, payment, and fulfillment. The themes are the order lifecycle state machine, a saga across services with compensation, inventory reservation, and reliable event-driven status updates.

nodes.label: Storefront / Client
nodes.description: Places orders and checks status; receives updates.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Routes order placement and status requests.

nodes.label: Order Service (Orchestrator)
nodes.description: Owns the order lifecycle state machine and saga across services.

nodes.label: Order Store
nodes.description: Authoritative orders + state transitions + idempotency, sharded by order id.

nodes.label: Inventory Service
nodes.description: Reserves and decrements stock; the authoritative availability.

nodes.label: Payment Service
nodes.description: Authorizes at order, captures on fulfillment.

nodes.label: Fulfillment Service
nodes.description: Routes to a warehouse, triggers pick/pack/ship.

nodes.label: Warehouse / WMS
nodes.description: Picks, packs, and ships; emits shipment events.

nodes.label: Event Outbox / Bus
nodes.description: Transactionally-written order events for downstream + notifications.

nodes.label: Notification Service
nodes.description: Emails/pushes order status to the customer.

nodes.label: Returns / Refund Service
nodes.description: Handles cancellations and returns; restocks + refunds.

nodes.label: Reconciler / Ops Worker
nodes.description: Reconciles ambiguous external steps (auth/capture/shipment timeouts), expires reservations, drains DLQ, and parks stuck orders for support.

functional: Place an order spanning multiple items; reserve inventory and take payment.
Drive the order through its lifecycle: placed -> paid -> fulfilled -> shipped -> delivered, with line-level state under the aggregate.
Orchestrate fulfillment (route to a warehouse, pick/pack/ship) and track shipment, including split shipments across warehouses.
Handle cancellations and returns — including partial cancel/return of some lines — restocking inventory and refunding payment.
Notify the customer at each status change.
nonFunctional: Consistency across services — never charge without reserving stock, never ship without payment.
No double-processing: order operations are idempotent (key scoped by customer + endpoint + request fingerprint; provider idempotency keys reused on retry).
Reliable: a crash mid-flow resumes; ambiguous external steps are reconciled, never blindly retried; stuck orders park for support.
Auditable: the full order history/state transitions and support actions are recorded.
Scale to millions of orders/day with many concurrent state changes.
Secure & private: authenticate customers, authorize order access, keep payment data tokenized (PCI boundary at the provider), protect PII, retain/expire it on policy.
Observable & operable: stuck-saga and outbox-lag metrics, retry/DLQ counts, payment/WMS error rates, and support dashboards for manual repair.

capacity.label: Orders/day
capacity.note: 1M/day ≈ 12 orders/s avg; a 10M peak day with a 3× intra-day spike ≈ 350 orders/s.

capacity.label: Items/order
capacity.note: At 350 orders/s that is ~1,000 line-level reservation writes/s (per SKU+warehouse, not one counter).

capacity.label: State writes/order
capacity.note: placed→paid→fulfilling→shipped→delivered ≈ 1,700 transition + history writes/s at peak.

capacity.label: Payment calls/order
capacity.note: ~700 payment provider calls/s at peak; refunds add a smaller tail.

capacity.label: Outbox events + callbacks
capacity.note: One outbox event per transition plus WMS shipment callbacks ≈ 2,000 published events/s at peak.

capacity.label: Storage/day (10M)
capacity.note: orders + order_items + order_events + outbox_events at a few KB/order; transition history and outbox kept hot ~30–90 days, then archived.

capacity.label: Sharding vs hot SKUs
capacity.note: Order-id sharding spreads order writes but does NOT relieve contention on a single popular SKU+warehouse row — that needs per-location stock, fan-out, or hold queues.

api.description: Place an order; reserves inventory and authorizes payment. Idempotent via Idempotency-Key (scoped customer+endpoint+fingerprint).
Get an order with line items, shipments, payment state, transition history, and pending compensations.
api.response: { "orderId": "o_9", "state": "partially_shipped", "items": [ {lineId, sku, lineState} ], "shipments": [ {shipmentId, tracking, state} ], "payment": { "state": "captured" }, "history": [ {from, to, ts} ], "pendingCompensations": [] }

api.description: Cancel an order or specific lines (only from cancellable states); releases reservations and voids/refunds. Idempotent.
Open a return for delivered lines; checks eligibility, then drives receive → restock decision → refund. Idempotent.
Provider callback for async payment results (auth/capture/refund). Dedupes on provider event id; resolves auth_pending/capture states.
Warehouse shipment event (packed/shipped/delivered). Dedupes and tolerates out-of-order delivery; advances the line and order state.

dataModel.name: orders
dataModel.note: Authoritative order header + aggregate lifecycle state + saga progress, sharded by order id.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(placed, paid, fulfilling, partially_shipped, shipped, delivered, cancelled, partially_cancelled, partially_returned, returned)

dataModel.name: order_items
dataModel.note: Line-level state and warehouse allocation; aggregate order state is derived from these.

fields.name: line_state
fields.type: enum(reserved, allocated, shipped, delivered, cancelled, returned)

dataModel.name: order_events
dataModel.note: Append-only history of state transitions (audit + resume + source for outbox).

dataModel.name: inventory_reservations
dataModel.note: Reservation per SKU+warehouse tied to a line item, with TTL and lifecycle.

dataModel.name: inventory
dataModel.note: Authoritative stock per SKU + warehouse (location, not a single global counter).

dataModel.name: payment_attempts
dataModel.note: Tracks provider operation IDs and states for auth/capture/refund + reconciliation.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(auth_pending, authorized, captured, capture_failed, refunded, voided)

dataModel.name: shipments
dataModel.note: Package-level fulfillment unit; one order can have several (split shipments).

dataModel.name: returns
dataModel.note: Return-after-delivery flow with eligibility, receipt, restock decision, and refund link.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Dedupes client requests; scoped by customer + endpoint + request fingerprint with a TTL.

dataModel.name: outbox_events
dataModel.note: Events written in the same txn as the transition; a relay publishes them at-least-once.

patterns.name: Order state machine
patterns.what: Model the order as explicit states with allowed transitions, persisted as the truth.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-step order workflows that must stay consistent and auditable.

patterns.name: Saga with compensation
patterns.what: Coordinate the cross-service flow with per-step compensating actions on failure.
patterns.whenToUse: Distributed workflows that can't be one ACID transaction.

patterns.name: Inventory reservation
patterns.what: Reserve stock at order time (atomic), decrement on ship, release on cancel.
patterns.whenToUse: Avoiding overselling across a multi-step order.

patterns.name: Idempotent transitions
patterns.what: Key each step (order id + transition) so retries don't double-process.

patterns.name: Transactional outbox
patterns.what: Write events in the same transaction as state changes; deliver reliably to consumers.
patterns.whenToUse: Reliable status updates / downstream sync without dual-write divergence.

patterns.name: Compensation on cancel/return
patterns.what: Undo completed steps (restock, refund) when an order is cancelled or returned.

steps.title: 1. Naive: One Synchronous Transaction (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest possible order placement: in a single request, the order service runs one synchronous flow — take the payment, decrement the stock, and mark the order shipped — ideally wrapped in one database transaction so it's all-or-nothing.

This is correct and trivial when everything lives in one database. But the moment payment, inventory, and fulfillment are separate services with separate stores (which they are), there is no single transaction to wrap them in. A crash or timeout after charging the card but before decrementing stock leaves the customer charged for an item that never ships, with no record of where the flow stopped.

So we reject this baseline deliberately — its value is the list of invariants the real design must preserve: never charge without reserved stock, never ship without payment, always have a durable resume point, and make every step idempotent. The rest of the design earns those back: model the order as an explicit, durable state machine, and coordinate the cross-service steps as a saga with compensation instead of pretending they're one ACID transaction.

view.caption: The storefront places an order through the API gateway to the order-service orchestrator, which persists to the order store and synchronously reserves stock and authorizes payment.

steps.decisionPrompt: You charge the card, then crash before decrementing stock. With payment and inventory in separate services, what undoes the charge?
whyNow: Trying the one-transaction version first makes the core problem unmistakable: the order spans independently-owned services, so you need a durable state machine plus compensation, not a single ACID transaction.

recap.after: Payment, stock, and shipping done in one synchronous in-process flow.
recap.newRisk: Payment, inventory, and fulfillment are separate services — there is no one transaction, so a partial failure leaves money and stock inconsistent with no resume point.

traps.trap: Wrapping the cross-service order flow in one database transaction.
traps.why: Payment, inventory, and fulfillment own separate stores; no single transaction spans them, and a crash mid-flow strands the order with no recovery point.
traps.instead: Persist an explicit order state machine and coordinate the steps as a saga with compensating actions.

steps.title: 2. The Order Lifecycle State Machine
description: Model the order as an explicit state machine: placed -> paid -> fulfilling -> shipped -> delivered, with cancel/return branches. The order store persists the current state and every transition as the source of truth.

Transitions are triggered by events and guarded. The allowed-transition matrix (trigger → guard) is the contract: placed → paid requires an authorized payment AND reserved inventory; paid → fulfilling requires successful warehouse routing; fulfilling → shipped requires a WMS shipment ack AND a successful capture (re-auth if expired); shipped → delivered requires a carrier delivery event; delivered → returned requires an accepted, eligible return; and any cancellable state (placed/paid/fulfilling, before ship) → cancelled releases the reservation and voids/refunds. Invalid transitions (e.g. ship an unpaid order, cancel an already-shipped line) are rejected by the guard, not just by convention.

Two levels of state coexist: the aggregate order state above, and per-line item state (reserved → allocated → shipped → delivered, with cancelled/returned branches) underneath it. The aggregate is *derived* from the lines — an order with some lines shipped and some pending rolls up to partially_shipped — so split shipments and partial cancel/return are first-class, not special cases.

This state machine is the backbone — inventory, payment, fulfillment, and notifications all hang off transitions, the append-only transition history gives auditability, and a crashed orchestrator resumes from the persisted state. 'What state is this order in' has exactly one authoritative answer.

view.caption: The storefront's order ops reach the order-service orchestrator through the API gateway, which persists each lifecycle state and idempotency key in the order store.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you ensure an order can never reach an inconsistent state (e.g. shipped but unpaid)?

concepts.definition: Explicit states + allowed transitions persisted as the source of truth.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees valid progressions, auditability, and crash-resume.
concepts.example: fulfilling -> shipped only after payment captured + warehouse ack.

concepts.definition: Each transition has a trigger and a guard the order store checks before applying it: placed→paid needs authorized payment AND reserved inventory; paid→fulfilling needs warehouse routing; fulfilling→shipped needs a WMS ship ack AND a successful capture (re-auth if expired); shipped→delivered needs a carrier delivery event; delivered→returned needs an accepted, eligible return; any pre-ship state→cancelled releases the reservation and voids/refunds.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes 'which transitions are legal from here' a checked contract, not a convention — impossible states (shipped-but-unpaid) are rejected by the guard.
concepts.example: Attempting fulfilling→shipped with an expired auth fails the guard until a successful re-auth + capture.

whyNow: The state machine is the consistency backbone of a multi-step order; every later mechanism triggers or reacts to its transitions.

recap.before: A single synchronous transaction with no resume point.
recap.after: A durable, auditable order lifecycle as the source of truth.
recap.newRisk: An order touches inventory, payment, and fulfillment — separate services, not one transaction.

traps.trap: Treating order state as a single display field updated wherever convenient.
traps.why: If any service can flip the state, guards aren't enforced and an order can reach impossible states (shipped-but-unpaid); resume and audit become unreliable.
traps.instead: Make the order store the only writer of state, validate every transition against the allowed-transition matrix, and append each transition to history.

traps.trap: Modeling only one order-level state for a multi-item order.
traps.why: Split shipments, partial cancellation, and partial returns can't be represented, so the aggregate state lies about what actually happened to each line.
traps.instead: Keep per-line item state and derive the aggregate (partially_shipped, partially_returned) from the lines.

steps.title: 3. Inventory Reservation
description: An order shouldn't be placed if items aren't available, and stock mustn't be oversold across the multi-step flow. Reserve inventory at order time: atomically check-and-reserve the quantities (decrement available, increment reserved) in the inventory service.

Reservation (not immediate decrement) holds the stock during the flow; it's decremented for real on ship and released back if the order is cancelled before shipping. This separates 'committed to this order' from 'shipped'. Each reservation carries a TTL so an abandoned checkout self-heals — an expiry worker releases holds that aren't confirmed in time.

The reserve is an atomic conditional update (reserve only if available >= qty), the same oversell-proof discipline as any limited-inventory system, applied per SKU and warehouse — inventory is location-scoped, so a multi-warehouse order reserves and allocates per line, not against a single global counter.

view.caption: The order-service orchestrator reserves stock via the inventory service, which atomically reserves or decrements the SKU in the inventory store.

options.name: Atomic conditional reservation (default)
options.description: Reserving stock is a single conditional UPDATE on the SKU+warehouse row — move qty from available to reserved only while available >= qty — so the database itself rejects oversell with no application lock. The real decrement happens on ship; the reservation releases on cancel/expiry.
pros: A single 'UPDATE SET available = available - q, reserved = reserved + q WHERE available >= q' atomically rejects oversell at the DB.
No application-level lock to manage — concurrency safety is the row's job.
Cheap and correct for the common case of two orders racing for the last unit; 'reserved' stays distinct from 'shipped'.
cons: A genuinely hot SKU+warehouse serializes all buyers on one row, capping throughput.
A reservation with no TTL strands stock if the order is abandoned mid-checkout — pair with an expiry worker.
Cross-warehouse allocation (which location fulfils which line) needs more than a single counter.

view.caption: The inventory service reserves stock with a single conditional decrement on the inventory store, letting the database reject oversell without an application lock.

options.name: Reservation hold with TTL
options.description: Stock is placed on a time-limited hold for the checkout window rather than decremented, and an expiry sweeper auto-releases holds that aren't confirmed in time.
pros: Reserves (not decrements) stock for the checkout window, then confirms or auto-releases on TTL expiry.
Abandoned carts don't permanently strand inventory — holds self-heal.
Separates 'reserved' from 'sold', giving accurate availability during long checkouts.
cons: Adds a hold table and an expiry sweeper to operate.
Available count is now (on-hand minus active holds), so reads must account for holds.
A too-short TTL frustrates slow shoppers; too long over-protects stock.

view.caption: The inventory service creates a time-limited hold in the inventory-and-holds store, which a sweeper auto-releases when the TTL lapses.

options.name: Pessimistic SELECT FOR UPDATE
options.description: The transaction locks the SKU row with SELECT FOR UPDATE for its duration and does an explicit read-modify-write, serializing every concurrent buyer of that item.
pros: Locks the SKU row for the duration of the transaction, giving the strongest serialization.
Conceptually simple: read-modify-write under an explicit lock.
Guarantees no two transactions touch the row concurrently.
cons: Holding a row lock across a multi-step reserve blocks every other buyer of that SKU.
Lock contention on a hot item causes timeouts and retry storms.
Worse throughput than an atomic conditional update for the exact same guarantee.

view.caption: The inventory service takes a SELECT FOR UPDATE row lock on the SKU in the inventory store, serializing concurrent buyers through a read-modify-write.

steps.decisionPrompt: Two orders want the last unit of a SKU at the same moment. How do you avoid overselling?

concepts.definition: Atomically reserve stock at order time; decrement on ship, release on cancel.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents overselling while holding stock across a multi-step order.
concepts.example: Reserve 1 unit -> ship decrements it -> cancel releases it.

whyNow: Reservation is the first cross-service step; getting atomic reserve + release right is what keeps the order from overselling or leaking stock.

recap.after: Stock reserved atomically at order time.
recap.newRisk: Reserve, pay, and fulfill span services — partial failures must not leave money/stock inconsistent.

steps.title: 4. Cross-Service Saga with Compensation
description: Placing an order spans inventory (reserve), payment (authorize), and later fulfillment — across separate services and databases. You can't wrap them in one ACID transaction, so coordinate a saga: a sequence of local steps, each with a compensating action if a later step fails.

The orchestrator (order service) drives the saga, persisting progress in the order state so it's resumable. If payment fails cleanly after reserving stock, the compensation releases the reservation. The subtle case is an ambiguous step: if authorization times out, you don't know whether the charge happened. Record auth_pending, wait for the provider webhook or poll the provider with the same idempotency key, and only then advance or compensate — never blindly retry, which risks a double charge.

Every step is idempotent: internal steps keyed by order id + step, and external calls reuse a stable provider idempotency key so a retry collapses onto the same auth/capture/refund. The state machine doubles as the saga's durable ledger.

view.caption: The order-service orchestrator drives the saga, reserving stock via the inventory service, authorizing/capturing the payment service, and persisting state in the order store.

options.name: Orchestrated saga (default)
options.description: A central order-service state machine drives each step in sequence and explicitly invokes compensations (release stock, void auth) when a later step fails.
pros: The Order Service drives each step and invokes explicit compensations (release stock, void auth) on failure.
Centralized state machine makes the whole flow auditable and easy to reason about.
Adding a step or a new compensation is a localized change in the orchestrator.
cons: The orchestrator is a central coordinator that must itself be reliable and idempotent.
Compensations are eventually consistent — there's a window where money is held but stock isn't.
More logic concentrated in one service.

view.caption: A central order-service state machine sequences the inventory and payment services, persisting to the order store and invoking compensations like release-stock or void-auth on failure.

options.name: Choreographed saga (events)
options.description: Services react to each other's events over an event bus with no central coordinator, and each domain owns and emits its own compensation when it must roll back.
pros: Services react to each other's events with no central coordinator — looser coupling.
Each domain owns its own compensation logic, so the order service stays thin.
Scales naturally: services subscribe to the event bus independently.
cons: The end-to-end flow is implicit, spread across subscribers — hard to trace and debug.
Cyclic event dependencies and ordering bugs are easy to introduce.
No single place shows 'where is this order in the saga' for support/audit.

view.caption: The order-service writes an event to the outbox/bus in the same transaction, and inventory and payment services react to OrderPlaced and StockReserved events with no central coordinator.

options.name: Distributed transaction (2PC)
options.description: A coordinator runs a two-phase prepare-then-commit across services so they all commit or all abort atomically, holding resource locks through the prepare phase.
pros: All-or-nothing across services in one atomic commit — no compensations to write.
No intermediate inconsistent state visible to anyone.
Strongest correctness guarantee on paper.
cons: A coordinator-held prepare phase locks resources across services, killing availability and throughput.
Payment gateways and external WMS simply don't speak 2PC, so it can't span them.
A coordinator crash mid-commit blocks participants indefinitely — a notorious failure mode.

view.caption: A two-phase-commit coordinator in the order service runs prepare-then-commit across the inventory and payment services so they commit or abort atomically.

steps.decisionPrompt: You reserved stock, then payment authorization fails. What has to happen?

concepts.definition: A multi-step distributed workflow where each step has a compensating undo on failure.
concepts.whyItMatters: Achieves cross-service consistency without distributed (2PC) transactions.
concepts.example: Reserve -> authorize fails -> compensate by releasing the reservation.

concepts.definition: Each saga step keyed so a retry is a no-op if already done.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes the saga safe to retry/resume after crashes.
concepts.example: Re-running 'reserve' for the same order doesn't reserve twice.

whyNow: The saga is the correctness heart of an OMS; orchestration + compensation + idempotency is exactly what an interviewer probes for multi-service consistency.

recap.after: A resumable, compensating saga keeping services consistent.
recap.newRisk: Once paid, the order must actually be fulfilled and shipped — orchestrating the warehouse.

traps.trap: Using a distributed (2PC) transaction across the services.
traps.why: Blocking, fragile, poor availability across independently-owned services.
traps.instead: An orchestrated saga with compensating actions and idempotent steps.

flows.name: Place-order saga: reserve -> authorize (with compensation)
flows.note: The orchestrator persists each step; if payment authorization fails after reserving stock, it compensates by releasing the reservation.

flows.name: Ambiguous authorization timeout: reconcile, don't retry
flows.note: Authorization times out — the charge may or may not have happened. The orchestrator parks the order at auth_pending and reconciles via webhook/poll using the same provider key before advancing or compensating.

steps.title: 5. Fulfillment Orchestration
description: Once paid, the order is fulfilled: the fulfillment service routes it to a warehouse (by location/stock), which picks, packs, and ships. The warehouse (WMS) is often external/async, so it emits shipment events back rather than responding synchronously.

Fulfillment is itself a saga sub-flow: capture payment on ship (not at order — you authorize at order, capture when it actually ships), decrement the reservation to a real decrement, and transition the order to shipped/delivered as shipment events arrive.
Split shipments and multi-warehouse routing add complexity (one order, several packages), each tracked as part of the order's fulfillment state.

view.caption: The order-service orchestrator triggers the fulfillment service to have the warehouse pick/pack/ship, receives shipment events back, and captures via the payment service.

options.name: Authorize at placement, capture at shipment (default)
options.description: Payment is authorized when the order is placed but the funds are only captured once the warehouse ships, and the auth is voided if the order is cancelled first.
pros: You verify funds up front but only take the money when goods actually ship — matches most consumer law and card-network rules.
If the order is cancelled before shipment, you void the auth with no refund flow.
Avoids charging for items that turn out to be unavailable at the warehouse.
cons: An authorization can expire (typically ~7 days) before a slow fulfillment ships, forcing re-auth.
Two payment interactions (auth then capture) instead of one.
Requires tracking auth state and handling capture failures at ship time.

view.caption: The order service authorizes payment at placement, lets the fulfillment service and warehouse ship, and captures the funds only on the returned shipment event.

options.name: Capture immediately at placement
options.description: The full charge is captured the moment the order is placed in one payment step, and any failure to fulfill is handled later by issuing a refund.
pros: Money is collected the moment the order is placed — simplest accounting and cash flow.
A single payment step, no auth-expiry tracking or re-auth.
Good fit for digital goods or instant-fulfillment items.
cons: If you can't fulfill, you must issue a refund — slower and costlier than voiding an auth.
Charging before shipment can violate card-network rules for physical goods.
Customer is out the money during any fulfillment delay or cancellation.

view.caption: The order service captures the full charge immediately via the payment service, then hands the order to fulfillment and the warehouse to pick/pack/ship.

steps.decisionPrompt: When do you capture the customer's payment — at order placement or at shipment? Why?

concepts.definition: Authorize payment at order; capture only when the item actually ships.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids charging for something that may never ship (out of stock, cancelled).
concepts.example: Authorize at placed; capture when the warehouse confirms shipment.

whyNow: Fulfillment is where the order becomes real; the authorize-at-order / capture-on-ship nuance and async warehouse events are senior signals.

recap.after: Warehouse-orchestrated fulfillment; capture on ship; tracked to delivery.
recap.newRisk: Customers and downstream systems need timely, reliable status — not lost updates.

traps.trap: Capturing payment at order placement for physical goods and refunding later if you can't ship.
traps.why: It charges the customer before anything ships (often against card-network rules), and a refund is slower and costlier than voiding an auth.
traps.instead: Authorize at order, capture only on the shipment event; re-authorize idempotently if the auth expired before a slow fulfillment ships.

traps.trap: Treating a WMS 'shipped' callback as a synchronous, exactly-once, in-order event.
traps.why: External warehouses deliver events asynchronously, duplicated, and out of order; applying them blindly double-captures or advances state wrongly.
traps.instead: Dedupe on the WMS event id, guard each transition against current line/order state, and ignore stale or duplicate callbacks.

flows.name: Shipment event -> capture -> consume reservation -> shipped
flows.note: The WMS shipment event drives capture and the real stock decrement. Capture failure or an expired auth re-authorizes (idempotently) instead of shipping unpaid.

steps.title: 6. Reliable Status Events (Outbox)
description: Every state change should notify the customer and feed downstream systems (analytics, fraud, loyalty). Don't publish events in a separate step from the state write — a crash between them lets the order state and what was announced diverge.

Use the transactional outbox: write the order event into an outbox table in the same transaction as the state transition. A relay polls/tails the outbox and publishes unsent rows to a broker; the notification service and other consumers read from the broker and dedupe on the event id. Each row tracks publish_status, attempts, and next_retry_at; a row stuck past N attempts goes to a DLQ and raises a stuck-row alert, and the event carries a payload_version so schemas can evolve.

This guarantees an event exists iff the transition committed — the customer never gets a 'shipped' email for an order that didn't actually transition. But be precise about the guarantee: the outbox makes committed events eventually published at-least-once; it does NOT make the downstream side effect (the email, the analytics write) exactly-once — consumers must still dedupe on the event id.

view.caption: The order service writes state to the order store and an event to the outbox/bus in one transaction, which delivers status events to the notification service and on to the storefront.

options.name: Transactional outbox (default)
options.description: The state change and its event row are written in one database transaction to an outbox table, and a relay polls the outbox and publishes unsent rows at least once.
pros: The state change and the 'order shipped' event are written in the same DB transaction, so they can't diverge.
A relay polls the outbox and publishes, giving exactly-the-event-happened-iff-state-changed semantics.
Survives a crash between commit and publish — the relay just retries unpublished rows.
cons: Adds an outbox table and a relay/poller process to operate.
Publish is slightly delayed by the polling interval.
Consumers must still dedupe, since the relay delivers at-least-once.

view.caption: The order service commits state and an event row to the outbox table in one transaction, and a relay publishes unsent rows at least once to the notification service.

options.name: Dual write (publish after commit)
options.description: The service commits the state change to the database and then directly calls the message bus to publish the event, with no atomicity linking the two writes.
pros: No outbox table or relay — just commit, then call the message bus.
Lowest-latency publish in the happy path.
Simplest code to write for a first version.
cons: A crash between the DB commit and the publish silently drops the event — the order is shipped but no one is told.
Or a publish that succeeds while the txn rolls back announces an event that never happened.
There is no atomicity across two systems, so divergence is inevitable under failure.

view.caption: The order service commits state to the order store and then separately publishes the event to the notification service, with no atomicity linking the two writes.

options.name: Change data capture (CDC) from the log
options.description: A CDC connector tails the database transaction log and emits an event for every committed row change, with no event-publishing code in the application's write path.
pros: Tails the database transaction log, so every committed state change emits an event with zero app code in the write path.
No outbox table to maintain — the WAL is the source of truth.
Captures all changes uniformly, useful for analytics and other consumers too.
cons: Couples the event schema to the physical table layout, making DB refactors riskier.
Requires running and operating CDC infrastructure (e.g. Debezium/connectors).
Harder to emit a clean domain event ('OrderShipped') vs a raw row change.

view.caption: A CDC log tailer reads the order store's write-ahead log and emits an event per committed change to the notification service, with no publish code in the write path.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why write the 'order shipped' event in the same transaction as the state change instead of publishing it after?

concepts.definition: Persist domain events within the business transaction; relay them afterward.
concepts.example: Transition + outbox row commit together; relay -> notify + downstream.

whyNow: Reliable, consistent status propagation is a core OMS expectation; the outbox is the standard guarantee and reuses the saga's atomicity.

recap.before: Events could diverge from order state.
recap.after: Status events emitted atomically, delivered at-least-once.
recap.newRisk: Orders get cancelled and returned — completed steps must be undone correctly.

traps.trap: Assuming the outbox makes the downstream side effect (email, analytics write) exactly-once.
traps.why: The relay delivers committed events at-least-once; a redelivered 'shipped' event can send a duplicate email if the consumer doesn't dedupe.
traps.instead: Treat the event id as a consumer dedupe key — the outbox guarantees a committed event is eventually published, the consumer guarantees it acts on it once.

traps.trap: Publishing the event after the commit in a separate step (dual write).
traps.why: A crash between commit and publish drops the event (shipped, nobody told); a publish that beats a rolled-back txn announces an event that never happened.
traps.instead: Write the event row in the same transaction as the transition and let a relay publish it; never cross two systems without atomicity.

steps.title: 7. Cancellations, Returns, and Scale
description: Cancellations and returns are compensation flows over the same saga, and they operate at the line level, not just the whole order. Cancel before shipping: release the inventory reservation and void/refund the payment for the cancelled lines. Return after delivery: check eligibility, receive the item, make a restock decision (restock / discard / refurbish), and refund — each step idempotent and recorded as order transitions.

Because lines carry their own state, an order can be partially shipped, partially cancelled, and partially returned while still rolling up to a coherent customer-facing summary state (e.g. partially_shipped, partially_cancelled, partially_returned). Refunds are per-line and reference the original payment attempt. Timing matters: a line can only be cancelled in cancellable states (not once shipped); returns kick off a reverse flow. The order state machine enforces which compensations are valid from the current line/order state.

Scale: shard orders by order id (per-order writes serialize locally); the orchestrator is stateless and resumes sagas from the durable order state; the outbox + async consumers scale independently. Everything is auditable via the transition history.

view.caption: The order-service orchestrator routes a cancel/return to the returns/refund service, which restocks via the inventory service and refunds via the payment service while state persists to the order store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A delivered order is returned. What gets undone, and how do you avoid double-refunding on a retry?

concepts.definition: Cancel/return operate per order line, not only per whole order, so an order can be partially cancelled or returned while other lines proceed.
concepts.whyItMatters: Real orders span items shipped from different warehouses at different times; whole-order-only compensation can't model partial outcomes.
concepts.example: Cancel one of three lines: release that line's reservation + refund its amount; the rest still ship. Order rolls up to partially_cancelled.

concepts.definition: On a return, a decision (restock / discard / refurbish) governs whether the returned unit goes back to sellable inventory.
concepts.whyItMatters: Not every returned item is resellable; refunding without the right disposition either oversells damaged goods or loses good stock.
concepts.example: Defective return -> refund issued, disposition = discard, inventory NOT incremented.

whyNow: Returns/cancellations complete the lifecycle as compensation flows; tying them to the state machine + idempotency shows the full reversible order is correct.

recap.after: Valid cancellations/returns via idempotent compensation; sharded, resumable, auditable.
recap.newRisk: Order-volume spikes and saga orchestration at scale — bounded by sharding, idempotency, and durable resume.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Persisted saga progress in the order state; resume idempotently on restart.
Shard by order id; stateless orchestrator; async outbox consumers.

bottlenecks.issue: Hot SKU+warehouse contention (order-id sharding doesn't help — it's a single stock row).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-warehouse stock rows, reservation hold queues, or sharded counters that reconcile; route lines across locations.

bottlenecks.issue: Stuck orders (provider outage, parked auth_pending, DLQ'd outbox row).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Reconciliation worker + support queue with metrics (stuck-saga age, outbox lag) and a manual-repair tool with audited actions.

failureDrills.scenario: A refund is issued, then the cancel request is retried.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The idempotency key recognizes the completed refund; no second refund occurs.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent compensation keyed by order id + step; provider refund reuses its idempotency key.

failureDrills.scenario: Payment authorization times out — unknown whether the charge landed.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Order parks at auth_pending; system reconciles via webhook/poll on the same provider key before advancing or compensating.
failureDrills.mitigation: Track provider intent/auth IDs; never blind-retry an ambiguous charge.

failureDrills.scenario: Capture fails at ship time, or the authorization has expired.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Re-authorize idempotently and capture; if it can't be collected, hold the shipment and park for support — never ship unpaid.
failureDrills.mitigation: Auth expiry tracked in payment_attempts; capture guarded by the state machine.

failureDrills.scenario: A WMS 'shipped' callback is duplicated or arrives out of order.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Dedupe on the WMS event id; apply only valid transitions, ignore stale/duplicate events.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent callback handler keyed by event id; guarded state transitions.

failureDrills.scenario: An outbox row fails to publish repeatedly.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The relay retries with backoff; after N attempts the row is DLQ'd and alerts; no committed event is silently lost.
failureDrills.mitigation: publish_status/attempts/next_retry_at on outbox_events + DLQ + outbox-lag alerting.

flows.name: Cancel/return: idempotent compensation
flows.note: A cancel runs the saga in reverse — restock and refund — keyed so a retried cancel never double-refunds.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Order Management System
finalDesign.description: The order service owns a durable lifecycle state machine (with line-level item/shipment state under the aggregate) and orchestrates a saga across inventory (atomic per-SKU+warehouse reservation with TTL), payment (authorize at order, capture on ship, tracked as payment attempts with provider operation IDs), and fulfillment (route to a warehouse that emits async, deduped shipment events). Each step is idempotent — internal steps keyed by order id + step, external calls reusing provider idempotency keys — and the order state doubles as the saga's resumable ledger; failures trigger compensating actions (release reservation, void payment). Ambiguous external steps (auth/capture/shipment timeouts) are reconciled by a reconciliation worker, not blindly retried, and stuck orders park for support. State transitions write events via a transactional outbox (relay → broker → deduping consumers, with retries/DLQ and lag alerts), delivered at-least-once to notifications and downstream consumers. Cancellations/returns are line-level compensation flows (restock + refund) valid only from appropriate states. Orders shard by id; the orchestrator is stateless and resumes from durable state; everything is auditable via transition history. (Outbox is drawn as a single node but is really table + relay + broker.)

view.caption: The storefront orders through the API gateway and order-service orchestrator, which persists to the order store, reserves inventory, charges via payments, drives fulfillment through the warehouse, emits status events via the outbox to notifications, and handles cancellations through the returns service; a reconciliation worker resolves ambiguous external steps, expires holds, and parks stuck orders.

flows.name: Happy path: place -> reserve -> authorize -> ship -> capture -> notify
flows.note: The end-to-end success flow. Authorize at placement, capture on the warehouse shipment event, consume the reservation, then publish status via the outbox.

flows.name: Payment ambiguity: reconcile, don't retry
flows.note: An auth or capture times out — the charge may or may not have landed. The order parks at auth_pending and the reconciliation worker resolves provider state (webhook or poll, same provider key) before advancing, retrying safely, or compensating.

flows.name: Cancel vs return: two branches
flows.note: Cancel before ship voids the auth and releases the reservation; return after delivery receives the item, restocks, and refunds — both idempotent.

functional.how: Order state machine with event-triggered transitions.

nonFunctional.how: State machine as durable saga ledger; stateless orchestrator.
Order-id sharding + async outbox consumers; per-warehouse stock relieves hot-SKU contention.
PSP tokenization keeps the PCI boundary at the provider; authn/authz on order access; PII retention/expiry; audited support actions.
Reconciliation worker + support queue; stuck-saga and outbox-lag metrics, retry/DLQ counts.

tradeoff: A relational store gives easy transactional writes for state + outbox + idempotency in one commit, but you shard it yourself.
A managed wide-column/NoSQL store (DynamoDB/Cosmos) auto-shards by order id and offers conditional writes, but multi-row transactional outbox is more awkward.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed auto-sharding store removes the need to hand-shard the order table by order id.

tradeoff: A row-per-SKU+warehouse RDBMS gives exact conditional decrements and durable reservations.
An in-memory store with atomic ops is faster for hot SKUs but needs a durable backing record for reservations.
A polling relay over the outbox table is simple and DB-only; CDC (Debezium) avoids a poller but couples to the table layout.
A log-based broker (Kafka) gives replay and many consumers; a managed queue (SQS/Pub/Sub) is simpler to operate but with weaker ordering/replay.
A workflow engine (Temporal/Step Functions) gives durable timers, retries, and resume out of the box.
A hand-rolled state machine in the order service keeps everything in one store but you build resume, timeout, and reconciliation yourself.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A durable workflow engine can absorb the saga ledger, retry timers, and reconciliation scheduling that the order service would otherwise hand-build.

tradeoff: A PSP (Stripe/Adyen/Braintree) handles tokenization, auth/capture/refund, idempotency keys, and the PCI boundary.
Direct acquirer integration gives control but pulls PCI scope and reconciliation in-house.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Using a PSP that tokenizes cards keeps raw PAN out of the OMS entirely, shrinking PCI scope to the token boundary.

say: Confirm an OMS orchestrating inventory, payment, fulfillment, returns.
Pin cross-service consistency and idempotency as hard goals.
Note it's not one ACID transaction.
Order lifecycle state machine as the source of truth.
Saga across inventory + payment with compensation.
Transactional outbox for reliable status events.
Tradeoffs: orchestration vs choreography, reservation TTLs, split shipments.
Mention multi-warehouse routing and partial fulfillment as extensions.

expectations: Models an order lifecycle and calls services.
May try one big transaction and miss compensation/idempotency.
Designs the state machine + saga with compensation.
Reasons about idempotent resumable sagas and ambiguous-step reconciliation.
Handles split shipments, returns, and scale precisely.

followUps: Orchestration (central) vs choreography (event-driven) saga — when would you pick each?
How do you handle an order split across multiple warehouses / shipments?
How do you reconcile an ambiguous payment or shipment step (timeout)?
How do reservation TTLs interact with abandoned/failed orders?
How would you support partial cancellation (some items, not the whole order)?
